answer:
Let the chessboard be an $8 \times 8$ grid. We can represent the colors of the squares as white (W) and black (B). Initially, the chessboard has a standard alternating pattern of white and black squares. We want to transform the chessboard such that the upper half (rows 1 to 4) are white and the lower half (rows 5 to 8) are black.

The initial coloring of the chessboard is such that the top-left square is white. The coloring pattern is W B W B W B W B, W B W B W B W B, ...
We can think of the chessboard as a matrix where each entry is either 0 (white) or 1 (black). The initial coloring is such that the top-left entry is 0 and the entries alternate between 0 and 1.

We are allowed to swap any two columns or any two rows. We want to transform the chessboard such that rows 1 to 4 are all 0 (white) and rows 5 to 8 are all 1 (black).

Let $r_i$ be the $i$-th row, where $i$ ranges from 1 to 8. We want to make $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4$ all 0 and $r_5, r_6, r_7, r_8$ all 1.
We can swap rows to move rows 1 to 4 to the bottom. This can be done by swapping rows 1 and 2, 2 and 3, 3 and 4. This requires 3 row swaps. Then we can swap rows 5, 6, 7, 8 to the top. This requires 4 row swaps.
However, we can also swap columns.
Let's consider the coloring of the chessboard. The first row is W B W B W B W B. The second row is B W B W B W B W. The third row is W B W B W B W B. The fourth row is B W B W B W B W. The fifth row is W B W B W B W B. The sixth row is B W B W B W B W. The seventh row is W B W B W B W B. The eighth row is B W B W B W B W.

We want to make the first four rows all white and the last four rows all black.
We can swap rows to move the first four rows to the bottom. This requires 4 row swaps. Then we can swap rows 5, 6, 7, 8 to the top. This requires 4 row swaps.
However, we can also swap columns.
We can swap columns to make the first four columns all white and the last four columns all black. This requires 4 column swaps.

We can consider the parity of the rows and columns. The chessboard has 8 rows and 8 columns. We want the first 4 rows to be white and the last 4 rows to be black. This means that the rows 1, 2, 3, 4 should have an even number of black squares, and the rows 5, 6, 7, 8 should have an odd number of black squares.
The number of black squares in each row is 4. So we want the first 4 rows to have an even number of black squares and the last 4 rows to have an odd number of black squares.
Since each row has 4 black squares, we want the first 4 rows to have an even number of black squares, and the last 4 rows to have an odd number of black squares.
This is possible by swapping rows.

Consider the coloring of the chessboard. The number of white squares is 32 and the number of black squares is 32.
We want to make the first 4 rows white and the last 4 rows black. This means we want to change the number of black squares in the first 4 rows to 0 and the number of black squares in the last 4 rows to 32.
We can swap rows to achieve this.

It is possible to make the upper half white and the lower half black.

\boxed{yes}
